title: The Stolen Jollof Rice
story: At the annual Lagos office Christmas party, someone stole the jollof rice — the entire tray. The suspects: Chidi, who was seen near the food table; Amara, who loudly declared she 'doesn't even like jollof'; and Emeka, who was caught on CCTV carrying a large foil-wrapped object to his car. The party started at 6 PM. The jollof disappeared at 7:15 PM. Chidi's badge shows he entered the kitchen at 7:12 PM. Amara was on a video call with her mother from 6:50 to 7:30 PM — three witnesses confirm. Emeka left the party at 7:20 PM.

questions.q: Who has the strongest alibi?
options: Chidi — he was in the kitchen but says he was getting ice
Amara — on a video call with witnesses
Emeka — he was in the parking lot
All alibis are equally weak
questions.explain: Amara was on a video call from 6:50-7:30 with three witnesses confirming. The jollof disappeared at 7:15 — she was on the call.

questions.q: What evidence is most damaging to Emeka?
options: He left at 7:20 PM
The CCTV showing a foil-wrapped object
He didn't say he liked jollof
His car was parked near the kitchen
questions.explain: CCTV showing him carrying a large foil-wrapped object is direct physical evidence. Leaving at 7:20 is suspicious timing but not proof by itself.

questions.q: If you were Defence for Chidi, what's your best argument?
options: He was getting ice from the kitchen freezer
Someone else could have taken it before 7:12
Both A and B together
He's a vegetarian
questions.explain: Combined: he had a legitimate reason (ice) AND the jollof could have been taken before he entered at 7:12. Multiple arguments are stronger than one.

questions.q: The jollof disappeared at 7:15. Chidi entered the kitchen at 7:12. Does this prove Chidi took it?
options: Yes — 3 minutes is enough time
No — he may have entered for another reason and the timing is circumstantial
Yes — nobody else was in the kitchen
No — Amara took it during her call
questions.explain: Timing alone is circumstantial. He could have entered the kitchen for ice (his claim) and the jollof could have been taken moments before. No direct evidence links him to the jollof specifically.

title: The Case of the Missing Exam Answers
story: Someone leaked the final exam answers at Unity University. Professor Okafor discovered the leak when 14 students all got question 7 perfectly right — a question he'd deliberately made nearly impossible. The answer key was stored in three places: his locked office, his laptop (password protected), and the department shared drive (accessed by 6 staff). The night before the exam, the janitor reports seeing a student 'fiddling with Professor Okafor's door.' CCTV in the hallway shows no one entering the office between 10 PM and 6 AM. The laptop was in his home. The shared drive access log shows someone logged in at 2 AM using Dr. Adebayo's credentials. Dr. Adebayo says she was asleep at 2 AM and her office was locked.

questions.q: The janitor saw a student at Prof Okafor's door, but CCTV shows no one entered. What's the most likely explanation?
options: The student picked the lock and CCTV missed it
The janitor was mistaken about which door
The student tried the door but it was locked and left
The CCTV was tampered with
questions.explain: If CCTV shows no entry, the student likely tried the door, found it locked, and left. The answers weren't taken from the office.

questions.q: The shared drive was accessed at 2 AM with Dr. Adebayo's credentials. Dr. Adebayo says she was asleep. What's the strongest inference?
options: Dr. Adebayo is lying — she leaked it herself
Someone else used Dr. Adebayo's credentials without her knowledge
The access log is wrong
Dr. Adebayo sleepwalks to her computer
questions.explain: Shared credentials are commonly stolen or borrowed. Someone could have her password. Without evidence she was awake, the safer inference is that someone used her credentials without consent.

questions.q: 14 students got question 7 right. Why is this significant?
options: It shows the exam was too easy
It's a statistical anomaly that suggests a leak specifically for that question
It proves all 14 cheated
It means question 7 should be removed from future exams
questions.explain: A deliberately near-impossible question being answered correctly by 14 students is a statistical red flag. It suggests the answer was shared, but doesn't prove which students received it vs genuinely figured it out.

questions.q: If you're prosecuting the student the janitor saw, what's your biggest weakness?
options: No CCTV evidence of entry
The janitor can't identify the student
The answers weren't taken from the office
All of the above
questions.explain: All three are major problems: no entry on CCTV, no identification, and the answers weren't even taken from the office. The case against this student is very weak.

title: The Great Wedding Cake Disaster
story: At the Adeyemi-Okonkwo wedding, the 5-tier custom cake collapsed during the reception. The cake cost ₦450,000. The baker, Mama Tola, says the venue's floor was uneven and the table wobbled. The venue manager says the table was fine and the DJ's bass vibrated the cake. The DJ says his speakers were on a separate stage 15 metres away. The wedding planner admits she placed the cake near the edge of the table. Photos from before the collapse show the cake leaning slightly to the left. The collapse happened at exactly 8:47 PM — the same moment the best man tripped over a cable near the cake table.

questions.q: Based on the evidence, what most likely caused the collapse?
options: Uneven floor — Mama Tola's claim
DJ's bass vibration — venue's claim
Cake placed near table edge + best man tripping
The cake was structurally flawed from the start
questions.explain: Photos show it was already leaning (placement issue) and the best man tripped near the table at the exact moment of collapse. Combined, these are the most direct causes.

questions.q: If Mama Tola (the baker) sues the venue for the uneven floor, what weakens her case?
options: The DJ's speakers were far away
Photos show the cake was already leaning before any floor issue
The best man was not a venue employee
The wedding planner admitted placing it near the edge
questions.explain: If the cake was already leaning before the collapse, it suggests the cake itself or its assembly was the problem — not just the floor. The lean is visible in photos, undermining the 'uneven floor' claim.

questions.q: The best man tripped at 8:47 PM — the exact moment of collapse. Is he liable?
options: Yes — his action directly caused the collapse
Likably not — the cake was already compromised (leaning) and placed at the edge
Yes — he should have watched where he was going
No — the venue should have protected the cable
questions.explain: While his trip was the trigger, the cake was already in a precarious state (leaning, near the edge). Liability would likely be shared or fall primarily on whoever placed it unsafely.

questions.q: If you're the wedding planner's lawyer, what's your best defence?
options: The baker should have warned about placement
The table was provided by the venue, not me
The best man caused the collapse, not my placement
I was not responsible for the cake setup
questions.explain: While the placement was suboptimal, the immediate cause was the best man's trip. However, the planner already admitted placing it near the edge, so this defence is weak — it shifts blame rather than addressing her own admitted negligence.

title: The Donkey That Won the Lottery
story: In 1889, a Texas rancher named Hiram Pemberton registered his donkey 'Justice' as the purchaser of a lottery ticket. Justice's ticket won $50,000 (a fortune at the time). Pemberton claimed the winnings, arguing that since he owned Justice and Justice 'bought' the ticket, the money belonged to him. The lottery commission refused to pay, saying animals cannot purchase lottery tickets. Pemberton's lawyer argued that Justice was a 'registered asset' and that Pemberton was claiming on behalf of his property. The case went to the Texas Supreme Court. The Chief Justice, known for his dry wit, asked: 'Can the donkey also be taxed on the winnings?' Pemberton's lawyer replied: 'Only if the donkey files a return, Your Honour.' The courtroom erupted in laughter.

questions.q: What is the core legal question in this case?
options: Whether donkeys can own property
Whether Pemberton can claim winnings from a ticket his donkey 'purchased'
Whether the lottery commission has the right to refuse payment
Whether animals have legal standing in Texas
questions.explain: The core issue is whether Pemberton can claim the winnings. The donkey can't own property, but the question is whether Pemberton (as owner) can claim through his animal's 'purchase.'

questions.q: Pemberton's lawyer argued Justice was a 'registered asset.' What's the logical flaw?
options: Donkeys are not assets
Being an asset doesn't mean the asset can make purchases
The lottery commission didn't recognise asset registration
Assets cannot win money
questions.explain: Even if Justice is an asset, assets don't have the legal capacity to make purchases. A car is an asset, but your car can't buy a lottery ticket. Legal capacity requires personhood.

questions.q: The Chief Justice's joke about taxing the donkey highlights what legal principle?
options: That animals can be taxed
That legal rights come with legal obligations
That the court has humour
That lottery winnings are always taxable
questions.explain: The joke underscores that you can't grant legal rights (like claiming winnings) without also imposing legal obligations (like paying taxes). If the donkey can't be taxed, it can't claim rights either.

questions.q: If this case were heard today, what would likely happen?
options: Pemberton wins — animals can own property through their owners
Pemberton loses — only legal persons can purchase lottery tickets
Settlement — the lottery pays a reduced amount
The case is dismissed for absurdity
questions.explain: Modern law is clear: only legal persons (humans or corporations) can enter contracts or purchase tickets. The donkey has no legal capacity. Pemberton bought the ticket himself, and the donkey story is a publicity stunt.

title: The Office Football Pool Fraud
story: Tunde ran the office football betting pool at a Lagos tech company. Every Monday, 30 employees paid ₦2,000 each into the pool, predicting weekend Premier League results. The winner took 70%, Tunde kept 10% for 'administration,' and 20% rolled over. Over 8 months, Tunde collected roughly ₦2 million. The problem: nobody ever won.每当 someone came close, Tunde would claim 'the rollover fund' was building up for a mega jackpot. When Adaeze asked to see the accounts, Tunde said they were 'on his personal phone' and he'd 'show her later.' He never did. When HR got involved, Tunde produced a spreadsheet showing the rollover had ₦800,000. The employees had contributed ₦1.6 million in that period. Tunde's 10% admin fee should have been ₦160,000.

questions.q: Employees contributed ₦1.6M. Tunde's 10% admin = ₦160K. Rollover should be 20% = ₦320K. Payouts to winners = 70% = ₦1.12M. But nobody won. How much should be in the rollover?
options: ₦800,000 — what Tunde claims
₦1.44M — the 70% payouts plus 20% rollover, since nobody won
₦320K — just the 20% rollover
₦1.6M — all of it, since nobody won anything
questions.explain: If nobody won, the 70% payout (₦1.12M) rolls into the pool too. So rollover = 70% + 20% = 90% = ₦1.44M. Tunde's 10% is ₦160K. He claims only ₦800K — a shortfall of ₦640K.

questions.q: Tunde refused to show the accounts. What does this suggest under Nigerian law?
options: Nothing — he has a right to privacy
Possible fraud or misappropriation of funds
He's just disorganised
The employees should have asked earlier
questions.explain: Refusing to show accounts when managing other people's money is a red flag for misappropriation. Under Nigerian criminal law, this could constitute obtaining by false pretence or criminal breach of trust.

questions.q: If you're prosecuting Tunde, what's your strongest piece of evidence?
options: The ₦640,000 shortfall between expected and claimed amounts
Nobody ever winning the pool
His refusal to show accounts
All of the above together
questions.explain: Combined, these form a powerful case: the mathematical shortfall proves misappropriation, nobody winning is suspicious, and refusing to show accounts demonstrates consciousness of guilt.

questions.q: If you're defending Tunde, what's your best argument?
options: The spreadsheet is accurate and the math is wrong
Tunde spent the admin fee on pool expenses (app, SMS notifications, etc.)
The rollover rules were never formally agreed in writing
He intended to pay it all back
questions.explain: If the rollover rules were never formally documented, Tunde could argue the payout structure was different than what employees assumed. It's the strongest because it attacks the premise of the prosecution's math.

title: The Cat That Inherited a Mansion
story: When eccentric billionaire Madame Dupont died in 2019, her will left her ₦2.3 billion mansion and estate to her cat, Monsieur Whiskers. The will stated: 'To my beloved Whiskers, who has been more loyal than any human, I leave my entire estate, to be managed by my butler, Jean-Pierre, for the care and comfort of said cat.' Madame Dupont's three children were left ₦1,000 each. The children sued, arguing that a cat cannot inherit property. Jean-Pierre, backed by a animal rights organisation, argued the will was valid and the cat's interests must be protected. The court had to decide: can an animal be a beneficiary of a will?

questions.q: What is the fundamental legal issue?
options: Whether Jean-Pierre is a trustworthy manager
Whether a cat can be a beneficiary of a will
Whether the children deserve more money
Whether the will was properly signed
questions.explain: The core issue is legal capacity — whether an animal can be a beneficiary. In most jurisdictions, animals cannot hold property directly, but trusts can be created for their care.

questions.q: In many jurisdictions, what's the typical solution to cases like this?
options: The cat gets the house outright
The will is void and children inherit everything
A trust is created for the cat's care, managed by a human trustee
The government seizes the estate
questions.explain: Most jurisdictions allow 'pet trusts' — a human trustee manages the funds for the animal's care. The animal doesn't own the property directly; the trust does, for the animal's benefit.

questions.q: Madame Dupont left her children ₦1,000 each. If the will is upheld, can they challenge it?
options: Yes — they can claim undue influence by Jean-Pierre
Yes — they can claim the testator lacked mental capacity
No — a will is a will
Both A and B are valid grounds for challenge
questions.explain: Children can challenge a will on grounds of undue influence (did Jean-Pierre pressure Madame Dupont?) or lack of testamentary capacity (was she of sound mind when she wrote it?). Both are standard grounds for will challenges.

questions.q: If you're the children's lawyer, what's your strongest argument?
options: Cats don't need mansions
Madame Dupont was clearly not of sound mind to leave everything to a cat
Jean-Pierre manipulated her into writing this will
The children are entitled to a fair share under inheritance law
questions.explain: Leaving ₦2.3 billion to a cat and ₦1,000 to three children is extreme. Arguing lack of testamentary capacity is the strongest — it directly challenges whether she was mentally competent to make such an unusual disposition.
